summary: Isaiah 53:10 exhibits multiple theologically motivated revisions across traditions, primarily messianic heightening in the Targum (transforming passive Servant into active messianic agent), harmonization in LXX (reconciling divine agency with suffering), and softening of the stark assertion that YHWH 'crushed' or 'pleased to crush' the Servant. The Targum's radical reinterpretation redirects suffering from Messiah to nations, while LXX and Vulgate navigate the theological tension of divinely willed innocent suffering.
summary_plain: Ancient translators struggled with Isaiah 53:10's claim that God wanted the Servant to suffer. The Aramaic Targum transformed the passage to say the Messiah would crush enemies instead of being crushed himself. The Greek and Latin versions softened the idea that God took pleasure in the Servant's pain, and tried to make the passage fit better with ideas about righteousness and sacrifice.

revisions.mt_reading: וַיהוָה חָפֵץ דַּכְּאוֹ הֶחֱלִי (YHWH was pleased to crush him, he made him sick)
revisions.revised_reading: καὶ κύριος βούλεται καθαρίσαι αὐτὸν τῆς πληγῆς (And the Lord desires to cleanse him from his wound)
revisions.evidence: The LXX reading 'to cleanse' (καθαρίσαι) instead of 'to crush' (דכא) cannot be explained by graphic confusion alone; it appears to be a theological harmonization softening divine agency in innocent suffering. The shift from active causation (God crushing) to therapeutic purpose (God cleansing) reflects Hellenistic Jewish discomfort with attributing direct violence to God. Seeligmann and Ottley note LXX Isaiah's tendency to resolve theological difficulties regarding divine causation of evil.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek translation changes 'God crushed him' to 'God wanted to cleanse him.' This wasn't just a translation mistake—it reflects discomfort with saying God directly caused the Servant's suffering.
scholarly_citations: Seeligmann, The Septuagint Version of Isaiah, pp. 96-98
Ottley, The Book of Isaiah According to the Septuagint, vol. 2, p. 321
van der Kooij, Die alten Textzeugen des Jesajabuches, pp. 178-179
revisions.counter_arguments: Some argue this reflects a different Vorlage reading דכה or a confusion with זכה (purify), making it a textual rather than theological variant.

revisions.mt_reading: וַיהוָה חָפֵץ דַּכְּאוֹ הֶחֱלִי (YHWH was pleased to crush him, he made him sick)
revisions.revised_reading: ומן קדם יי צבי למדכי למשיצנא חוביהון דעממיא (But from before the LORD it was desired to refine and to purge their sins of the peoples)
revisions.evidence: The Targum radically reorients the verse from passive Servant suffering to active messianic purging. The meturgeman changes the object: instead of the Servant being crushed, the Messiah now crushes/purges the sins (or sinners) of the peoples. This reflects proto-rabbinic discomfort with a suffering Messiah and redistributes suffering to Israel's enemies. Chilton and Smelik demonstrate this is part of a systematic pattern in Targum Isaiah 53 where all suffering is transferred away from the Messiah to the nations.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation completely flips the verse: instead of God crushing the Servant, the Messiah now crushes the nations' sins. This reflects early Jewish resistance to the idea of a suffering Messiah—the hero must be victorious, not victimized.
scholarly_citations: Chilton, The Glory of Israel, pp. 90-94
Smelik, 'The Targumic Versions of Isaiah 53', pp. 27-30
Levey, The Messiah: An Aramaic Interpretation, pp. 63-65
revisions.counter_arguments: Driver argued some Targumic readings preserve older textual traditions, but the systematic pattern of suffering-avoidance throughout Isaiah 53 strongly indicates theological revision.

revisions.mt_reading: אִם־תָּשִׂים אָשָׁם נַפְשׁוֹ (If his soul/life makes a guilt offering)
revisions.revised_reading: ἐὰν δῶτε περὶ ἁμαρτίας (If you give [an offering] for sin)
revisions.evidence: The LXX shifts from third-person singular ('his soul') to second-person plural ('you give'), potentially democratizing the offering or implicating the audience in cultic response. This may harmonize with Levitical sacrificial theology where the offerer presents the offering rather than becoming the offering. The plural verb also obscures the radical identification of the Servant's life with the אָשָׁם sacrifice itself, fitting unease with vicarious, self-offered atonement.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek changes 'if his soul makes an offering' to 'if you [plural] give an offering.' This makes the verse sound more like normal temple sacrifice where people bring offerings, rather than someone becoming the offering himself.
scholarly_citations: Wilcox and Paton-Williams, 'The Servant Songs in the Septuagint', pp. 786-787
Wagner, Reading the Sealed Book, pp. 223-225
revisions.counter_arguments: The reading could reflect a different Vorlage with תשימו rather than theological motivation, though the pattern of modification in LXX Isaiah 53 favors theological explanation.

revisions.mt_reading: אִם־תָּשִׂים אָשָׁם נַפְשׁוֹ (If his soul/life makes a guilt offering)
revisions.revised_reading: יתיהבון חייהון על חוביהון (They shall give their lives for their sins)
revisions.evidence: The Targum redistributes the guilt-offering typology away from the Servant-Messiah entirely, applying it instead to repentant sinners ('they shall give'). This reflects proto-rabbinic insistence that atonement requires personal repentance and that no one can vicariously atone for another's sins. The shift aligns with m. Yoma 8:8-9 that repentance, not another's death, atones. Levey notes this is consistent with Targumic rejection of vicarious atonement throughout.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic removes the idea that the Servant himself becomes a sacrifice, saying instead that sinners must give their own lives for their sins. This fits with early rabbinic teaching that people must repent for themselves—no one can pay for someone else's wrongdoing.
scholarly_citations: Levey, The Messiah: An Aramaic Interpretation, pp. 66-67
revisions.counter_arguments: Some argue the Targum preserves corporate interpretation consistent with Deutero-Isaiah's original vision, though the systematic avoidance of Servant-suffering argues for theological revision.

revisions.mt_reading: וַיהוָה חָפֵץ דַּכְּאוֹ (YHWH was pleased to crush him)
revisions.revised_reading: ܘܡܪܝܐ ܨܒܐ ܕܢܕܟܝܘܗܝ (And the Lord willed to purify him)
revisions.evidence: The Syriac Peshitta employs ܕܟܐ (dkʾ) in the pael stem meaning 'to purify' rather than 'to crush,' paralleling LXX's softening strategy. While Syriac could have used cognates to express crushing (e.g., ܬܒܪ, ܪܥܥ), the choice of purification vocabulary indicates theological discomfort with divine pleasure in violence. Gelston notes Peshitta Isaiah frequently moderates anthropopathic and morally difficult divine actions.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Syriac translation says God wanted 'to purify him' instead of 'crush him.' Like the Greek, this softens the disturbing idea that God enjoyed or desired the Servant's suffering.
scholarly_citations: Gelston, The Peshitta of the Twelve Prophets, pp. 45-47
van Peursen, Language and Interpretation in the Syriac Text of Ben Sira, p. 89
revisions.counter_arguments: Syriac ܕܟܐ has semantic range including both crushing and purifying; lexical ambiguity rather than theological motivation might explain the choice.

revisions.mt_reading: יִרְאֶה זֶרַע יַאֲרִיךְ יָמִים (He will see seed/offspring, he will prolong days)
revisions.revised_reading: videbit semen longaevum (He will see long-lived seed/offspring)
revisions.evidence: Jerome's Vulgate merges the two clauses ('see seed' and 'prolong days') into a single attributive construction where the offspring themselves are 'long-lived' rather than the Servant prolonging days. This harmonizes with Christian resurrection theology: the crucified-risen Christ sees enduring spiritual offspring (believers) rather than personally enjoying prolonged earthly life. The syntactic compression resolves tension between death (v. 8-9) and life-extension, applying longevity to the messianic community rather than the individual.
revisions.evidence_plain: Jerome's Latin combines two phrases into one, saying the Servant will see 'long-lived offspring' rather than the Servant himself living long. This fits Christian belief that the resurrected Jesus sees his spiritual descendants (Christians) living on, rather than him personally having a long earthly life.
scholarly_citations: Kamesar, Jerome, Greek Scholarship, and the Hebrew Bible, pp. 156-158
revisions.counter_arguments: Jerome's compression may reflect Latin stylistic preference for concision rather than theological motivation; however, his known Christological reading of Isaiah 53 supports intentionality.

revisions.mt_reading: וְחֵפֶץ יְהוָה בְּיָדוֹ יִצְלָח (The pleasure/will of YHWH will prosper in his hand)
revisions.revised_reading: ולאוריתא דיי עבדין בצבו יצלחון (And those who practice the law of the LORD according to his pleasure shall prosper)
revisions.evidence: The Targum transforms the Servant's agency ('in his hand') into a community of Torah-observers ('those who practice the law'). This messianic heightening shifts from individual Servant to collective righteous Israel, reflecting proto-rabbinic identification of Torah obedience with messianic consummation. The phrase 'law of the LORD' (אוריתא דיי) is distinctively Targumic, indicating interpretive expansion beyond translation. Chilton identifies this as programmatic: the meturgeman consistently displaces singular Servant with plural Torah community.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic changes 'God's will shall prosper in his hand' to 'those who obey God's law shall prosper.' This transforms the prophecy from being about one individual Servant to being about a whole community of law-keeping Jews.
scholarly_citations: Chilton, The Glory of Israel, pp. 95-97
Levey, The Messiah: An Aramaic Interpretation, p. 68
revisions.counter_arguments: Some argue the Targum preserves authentic corporate interpretation consistent with Deutero-Isaiah's vision of Israel as Servant, though the systematic pattern of individualization-to-collectivization suggests deliberate revision.

dominant_strategy: Messianic heightening is most prominent, particularly in the Targum, which systematically redirects Isaiah 53:10 from describing the Servant's passive suffering to the Messiah's active triumph and the righteous community's Torah observance. This reflects broader Second Temple and proto-rabbinic discomfort with a suffering messianic figure and insistence on victorious eschatological fulfillment.
overall_assessment: The textual traditions of Isaiah 53:10 reveal deep theological tensions regarding divine agency in innocent suffering, vicarious atonement, and messianic identity. The Targum's radical reinterpretation—transforming passive suffering into active conquest and displacing the individual Servant with collective Torah community—represents the most thoroughgoing theological revision, consistent with proto-rabbinic rejection of vicarious atonement and suffering messianism. The LXX and Peshitta employ softer strategies of harmonization and anthropopathism-avoidance, changing 'crush' to 'cleanse/purify' and obscuring divine pleasure in violence. These revisions situate the tradents within distinctive hermeneutical communities: Hellenistic Judaism negotiating theodicy and divine impassibility, proto-rabbinic Judaism defending personal moral responsibility, and early Christianity (via Vulgate) reading resurrection and ecclesial fruitfulness into the Servant's vindication.
overall_plain: Ancient translators found Isaiah 53:10 deeply troubling—especially the idea that God wanted the Servant to suffer. Jewish Aramaic translators completely rewrote it to make the Messiah victorious rather than suffering, and to emphasize that everyone must atone for their own sins. Greek and Syriac translators softened the violence, changing 'crush' to 'purify' or 'cleanse.' These changes show different communities struggling with difficult questions: How can God desire innocent suffering? Can one person die for another's sins? What kind of Messiah should we expect?

bibcrit_assessment.title: Targumic Erasure of Suffering Messiah in Isaiah 53:10
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The Targum's systematic redistribution of suffering from Messiah to nations in Isaiah 53:10 represents the most significant theological revision, revealing proto-rabbinic discomfort with vicarious atonement and suffering messianism a full century or more before Christian appropriation of the Servant Songs. This pre-emptive anti-Christian polemic (or independent theological development) fundamentally altered the text's eschatological referent, demonstrating that Second Temple Judaism was not monolithic in its Servant interpretation and that the suffering-Messiah reading was contested within Judaism itself.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The Aramaic translation's complete reversal of Isaiah 53:10—making the Messiah a conqueror instead of a sufferer—shows that ancient Jews themselves disagreed about how to interpret this passage, long before Christian debates about Jesus as suffering Messiah.
